fact_texts: John Hammerton wrote a formal letter to his grandmother on June 1st 1833 from Shaw, addressed to a Dear Madam, expressing hope for a favorable answer and will wait upon her on Monday, promising a steady, sober married life, noting his custom to write the family name with a double 'm', and indicating his intention to reform his way of life
John Hammerton's mother died at age 24, left all her property to him, and her early death caused melancholy over his childhood and led to his father's loss of interest in his profession
After his wife's death, John Hammerton's father lived alone with servants, had little interest in his profession, and had no desire to accumulate wealth.
John Hammerton's father was physically strong, believed he could drink deeply without harm, but years of brandy and tobacco consumption weakened his constitution and led to gradual health decline.

John Hammerton's father was a lawyer with a lucrative practice in 1834, received a testimonial from client Mr. Waring for services in a case, but after his wife's death in September 1834, his practice suffered and he sought relief from depression in society of lively companions
John Hammerton has no relations near him, no family ties, no domestic comforts, has lost inclination for keeping company of young men, and is looking for a steady sober married life
The grandfather had an estrangement with his brother James Hamerton, the squire of Hellifield Peel, due to a land disagreement, which prevented family visits.
In 1876, John Hammerton found a lock of his mother's hair in a legal document, which he now possesses

The narrator's grandfather, Gilbert Hamerton, a second son of an ancient Yorkshire family, lived in a large old house near Towneley Park, owned property in the town and elsewhere, and married Jane Smith, a farmer's daughter, in an imprudent but socially acceptable union.
After his wife's death, John Hammerton's father lived alone with servants, had little interest in his profession, and had no desire to accumulate wealth.
John Hammerton has only one or two formal compositions written by his mother at school
